title: Collaboration inherits chat's full-node, mutable-stamp, and signer-mining constraints
content:
## Inherited constraints from the chat stack

Because collab builds on the same Swarm primitives as chat (PSS for invitations, GSOC for real-time pings, ACT for access control, SwarmChannel for push/pull), it inherits the same operational constraints listed in the design's Risks section:

- **Full-node requirement.** Both sender and receiver need full Bee nodes to use GSOC. Same constraint as chat. The UI degrades to polling-only for light nodes — slower but functional, not broken.
- **Mutable stamp requirement.** GSOC requires mutable stamps. Already the default in stamp selection; the adapter should verify before enabling collab on a target.
- **Signer mining time (10–30 s).** Mining a GSOC signer targeting a peer's neighborhood is paid once per (peer, collab) pair on collab creation/join. Background-mine; surface a "connecting" hint on the collab row; fall back to polling until mining completes.
- **ACT revocation semantics.** Removing a participant rebuilds the ACT chain via `patchGrantees`. Old content remains decryptable by anyone who already had it — you can't unring a bell. Acceptable for the drive/doc case and documented in UI.
- **Op bursts.** A drag-reorder on a large list could emit dozens of ops per second. Coalesce GSOC pings per (peer, collab) with a ~300 ms debounce.

These constraints are load-bearing assumptions — regressions in chat's underlying stack will break collab in the same way.
description: Collab inherits the same chat-stack constraints: both participants need full Bee nodes for GSOC, mutable stamps are required, and signer mining takes 10-30s per peer.
